title: Add concurrent order processing with DB transactions
description: Implements concurrent order processing using database transactions for inventory reservation.
diff:
diff --git a/services/inventory_service.py b/services/inventory_service.py
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..d6e7f8a
--- /dev/null
+++ b/services/inventory_service.py
@@ -0,0 +1,22 @@
+from __future__ import annotations
+from database import get_session
+
+class InventoryService:
+    def reserve_stock(self, product_id: int, quantity: int) -> bool:
+        session = get_session()
+        # Transaction 1: Read current stock
+        product = session.execute(
+            "SELECT stock FROM products WHERE id = %s", (product_id,)
+        ).fetchone()
+        
+        if product.stock < quantity:
+            return False
+        
+        # Transaction 2: Update stock
+        # Deadlock risk: concurrent requests can interleave reads and writes
+        session.execute(
+            "UPDATE products SET stock = stock - %s WHERE id = %s",
+            (quantity, product_id)
+        )
+        session.commit()
+        return True
comments: yuri: Works for single-threaded processing. Approve.

zara: Classic lost-update problem. Two concurrent requests can both read stock=5, both see it's enough for quantity=3, and both decrement. Final stock would be -1. Use SELECT ... FOR UPDATE or optimistic locking with a version column.
adam: Under load this will also cause deadlocks when two transactions lock rows in different orders across related tables.
author-beth: I'll add FOR UPDATE to the SELECT.
author: beth
